architecture.summary: This is a survey paper that systematically categorizes and summarizes prompt engineering techniques for large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs). It does not propose a novel system or mechanism, but rather documents existing prompting methods (zero-shot, few-shot, chain-of-thought variants, retrieval-augmented generation, code-based reasoning, etc.) and organizes them by application domain. The paper provides a taxonomy diagram and tables mapping techniques to models, datasets, and evaluation metrics.

biblio.title: A Systematic Survey of Prompt Engineering in Large Language Models: Techniques and Applications

claims.statement: Prompt engineering has emerged as an indispensable technique for extending the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs), allowing task-specific instructions to enhance model efficacy without modifying core model parameters.
Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting achieved state-of-the-art performance in math and commonsense reasoning benchmarks, achieving an accuracy of 90.2% on PaLM 540B.

Self-Consistency with Chain-of-Thought prompting resulted in accuracy improvements of 17.9% on GSM8K, 11.0% on SVAMP, 12.2% on AQuA, 6.4% on StrategyQA, and 3.9% on ARC-challenge compared to the baseline chain-of-thought prompting.
Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) prompting achieved a 74% success rate on Game of 24 tasks compared to CoT's 4%, and achieved a 60% success rate in word-level tasks versus CoT's 16%.
Chain-of-Symbol (CoS) prompting elevated ChatGPT's performance from 31.8% to 92.6% accuracy on Brick World tasks and achieved up to 65.8% reduction in prompt tokens.

Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) outperformed seq2seq models and task-specific architectures on ODQA benchmarks, achieving exact match scores up to 56.8% on TriviaQA and 44.5% on Natural Questions.

The survey analyzes over 41 distinct prompt engineering techniques categorized by application area, including zero-shot prompting, few-shot prompting, chain-of-thought variants, retrieval-augmented generation, code-based reasoning, and optimization strategies.

Few-shot prompting requires additional tokens to include examples, which may become prohibitive for longer text inputs, and the selection and composition of prompt examples can significantly influence model behavior with potential biases.
